players.name: Juan Musso
players.bio: Shot-stopper with safe hands and sharp reflexes, Musso has been a reliable presence at Atlético Madrid. With just 4 caps, this World Cup is his golden chance to step out from Dibu's giant shadow.
players.birth_location: San Nicolás de los Arroyos, Argentina
players.facts: Born in San Nicolás de los Arroyos, he's more used to being third-choice for Argentina than third-choice for his club.

players.name: Marcos Senesi
players.bio: Composed and combative, Senesi built his reputation in the Eredivisie before heading to the Premier League with Bournemouth. A cool-headed defender who reads the game well beyond his modest cap count.
players.facts: Shares a birthday month with fellow defender Tagliafico — May must be a great month for Argentine left-footers.

players.name: Nicolás Tagliafico
players.bio: A World Cup winner who has been a dependable servant on the left side for over a decade. Tagliafico is the kind of full-back who never seeks the limelight but always does the dirty work. A true soldier of the Albiceleste.
players.facts: He scored Argentina's equaliser vs France in the 2021 Copa América semi-final. 76 caps and barely a fuss made — pure professionalism.

players.name: Gonzalo Montiel
players.bio: The man who scored THAT penalty in Qatar 2022 to win Argentina the World Cup. Montiel tucked it away with ice in his veins, and returned to River Plate as a legend. His right foot changed history.
players.facts: Gonzalo Montiel's penalty against France in the 2022 World Cup final is arguably the most important kick in Argentine football history.

players.name: Leandro Paredes
players.bio: A deep-lying playmaker who controls tempo with elegant precision, Paredes returned to boyhood club Boca Juniors after spells at PSG and Juventus. A composed metronome in Argentina's midfield engine room.
players.facts: Paredes is a childhood friend of Lionel Messi — the pair grew up playing together in the Argentine youth system.

players.name: Lisandro Martínez
players.bio: Nicknamed 'The Butcher of Gualeguay', Martínez is a ferociously competitive centre-back who wins headers, tackles and, occasionally, enemies. A Manchester United stalwart when fit, he's fierce at international level too.
players.kit: Nike Mercurial Superfly VII Elite FG
players.facts: His nickname 'The Butcher of Gualeguay' was coined by Manchester City fans — they meant it as an insult. He wears it as a badge of honour.

players.name: Rodrigo De Paul
players.bio: The engine, the enforcer, and Messi's midfield shadow. De Paul covers every blade of grass and brings tenacity to Argentina's heart. Now plying his trade at Inter Miami alongside his captain, the bromance continues.
players.facts: De Paul and Messi are famously inseparable — teammates at Inter Miami and apparently also on group chats, holidays, and possibly grocery runs.

players.name: Valentín Barco
players.bio: At just 21, Barco is one of the most exciting young talents in South America. The attack-minded left-back has pace, technique, and an eye for goal that belies his age. The future of Argentina's left flank is bright.
players.facts: Born in a town literally called '25 de Mayo' — even his birthplace sounds like a national holiday. Two international goals at 21 is no coincidence.

players.name: Julián Alvarez
players.bio: The Spider from Calchín. Julián Álvarez announced himself to the world at Qatar 2022 with four goals and boundless energy. Now thriving at Atlético Madrid, the relentless forward is Argentina's heir apparent up front.
players.facts: Álvarez scored four goals at the 2022 World Cup despite barely being 22. He also hails from Calchín — a town of roughly 1,500 people.

players.name: Lionel Messi
players.bio: The greatest of all time. Eight Ballon d'Or awards, a World Cup, Copa América titles — Messi has done it all. At 38, this may be his final World Cup, and the world will watch every touch with reverence and a little heartache.
players.facts: Messi has scored 117 international goals. To put that in perspective, some nations haven't scored that many in their entire World Cup history.

players.name: Giovani Lo Celso
players.bio: A silky, technically gifted attacking midfielder who has never quite fulfilled his enormous potential due to injuries. When fit, Lo Celso is a joy — creative, clever, and capable of the unexpected. Real Betis love him dearly.
players.facts: Lo Celso and Messi both hail from Rosario — Argentina's most productive footballing city by output per square kilometre.

players.name: Gerónimo Rulli
players.bio: An experienced shot-stopper who has kept goal for clubs across Europe including Villarreal, Lazio, and Marseille. Rulli is a reliable backup whose penalty heroics in the 2021 Champions League final remain etched in memory.
players.facts: Rulli saved the decisive penalty in Villarreal's 2021 Champions League semi-final shootout vs Arsenal — then missed in the final. Drama follows him.

players.name: Cristian Romero
players.bio: Cuti Romero is a force of nature — aggressive, athletic and utterly uncompromising. The Tottenham centre-back brings chaos to opposition attacks and order to Argentina's defence. Not a player you want to face in a dark alley.
players.facts: Romero has received more yellow cards per game than almost any top-level defender in Europe. He considers this a compliment.

players.name: Exequiel Palacios
players.bio: A dynamic box-to-box midfielder with a powerful engine, Palacios developed through River Plate before thriving at Bayer Leverkusen's title-winning side. Tenacious and mobile, he adds steel to Argentina's midfield.
players.facts: Palacios was part of Bayer Leverkusen's historic unbeaten Bundesliga title-winning season in 2023-24 — an astonishing 51 games without defeat.

players.name: Nicolás González
players.bio: A versatile and direct winger with pace and a strong left foot, Nico González can play across the front line. Now reunited with Julián Álvarez at Atlético Madrid, he offers Scaloni a dangerous option from the wide right.
players.facts: Nico González shares his birthday — 6th April — with right-back Nahuel Molina. Same squad, same birthday. Make of that what you will.

players.name: Thiago Almada
players.bio: A hugely exciting creative talent, Almada caught the world's attention in MLS before earning his move to Atlético Madrid. Quick, technically brilliant, and already a fan favourite, he could be a breakout star at this tournament.
players.facts: Almada won MLS Cup with Columbus Crew and Atlanta United before age 24 — an impressive haul before even reaching Europe's top level.

players.name: Giuliano Simeone
players.bio: Son of Atlético Madrid's legendary manager Diego Simeone, Giuliano has had to carve his own path. A lively, hard-working forward, he chose Argentina over Italy and is determined to make the shirt his own.
players.facts: Born in Rome to Diego Simeone, Giuliano was eligible for Italy but chose Argentina. El Cholo must've had a quiet word at dinner.

players.name: Nico Paz
players.bio: Son of ex-Real Madrid midfielder David Paz, Nico was born in Tenerife but plays for Argentina. A wonderfully gifted attacking midfielder at Como, he's one of Serie A's most exciting young talents at just 21.
players.facts: Nico Paz was born in Spain, raised in football royalty, and plays in Italy — yet proudly wears the Albiceleste. The most international of Argentines.

players.name: Nicolás Otamendi
players.bio: The warrior of Benfica and Argentina's defensive rock for over a decade. Otamendi has seen it all — Premier League battles, World Cup heartbreaks, and ultimately glory in Qatar 2022. Still going at 38, still magnificent.
players.facts: Otamendi has 132 caps — only Messi has more for Argentina. He also won the 2021 Copa América and 2022 World Cup. A full trophy cabinet at last.

players.name: Alexis Mac Allister
players.bio: Born on Christmas Eve and a gift to football ever since. Mac Allister is composed, intelligent, and technically superb — a key cog in Liverpool's title-winning machine and a player who makes everything around him look easy.
players.facts: Mac Allister is one of three footballing brothers — all professionals. His father and uncle played too. Football is simply the family business.

players.name: José Manuel López
players.bio: A physical, hard-working centre-forward who leads the line with commitment if not yet consistent output at international level. López has impressed at Palmeiras and arrives at his first World Cup eager to make an impression.
players.facts: López plays for Palmeiras in Brazil — an Argentine striker plying his trade in enemy territory. That takes a certain kind of confidence.

players.name: Lautaro Martínez
players.bio: The Bull from Bahía Blanca. Lautaro is Argentina's alpha striker — clinical, combative, and capable of moments of brilliance. Serie A's top scorer and Inter Milan's talisman, he and Messi are a devastating partnership.
players.facts: Lautaro has scored 37 goals in 77 caps — a ratio that rivals the very best strikers in international football history.

players.name: Emiliano Martínez
players.bio: Dibu. The pantomime villain turned bona fide legend. Martínez's penalty shootout heroics are the stuff of Argentine folklore. Eccentric, brilliant, and utterly unhinged in the best possible way. Opposing penalty-takers beware.
players.facts: Dibu's Golden Glove celebration at Qatar 2022 — well, let's just say it was not entirely what FIFA had in mind. He was fined. He didn't care.

players.name: Enzo Fernández
players.bio: Won the World Cup as a 21-year-old and hasn't stopped since. Enzo Fernández is a complete central midfielder — powerful, technical, and a fierce competitor. Chelsea paid £107m for him. Most days, he looks worth every penny.
players.facts: Fernández became the most expensive British transfer ever at £107m to Chelsea in January 2023 — months after winning a World Cup. Quite the year.

players.name: Facundo Medina
players.bio: A rugged, no-nonsense centre-back who has impressed in Ligue 1 with Marseille. Medina is a physical presence in the air and a reliable defender. His nine caps suggest squad depth, but don't mistake that for lack of quality.
players.facts: Medina plays alongside Rulli at Marseille — so the Argentine goalkeeping rivalry continues at club level too. Must make for interesting training.

players.name: Nahuel Molina
players.bio: An overlapping right-back with pace, technical quality, and a nose for the big moment. Molina has been Argentina's first-choice on the right flank for years, and his energy and deliveries remain a key weapon for Scaloni.
players.facts: Molina shares his 6th April birthday with Nico González — two Argentina starters born on the same day, four years apart. The stars aligned.
